data.title: Materiality Matters: The ESG Factors That Count
data.description:
By Patrick O'Connell, CFA, John Huang, CFA and Erin Bigley, CFA

The materiality of ESG factors differs across sectors and markets. Investors need to understand how.

As environmental, social and governance (ESG) factors help contribute to—or detract from—security returns, it makes sense for active investors to integrate them into security selection. But there’s a wide disparity in the materiality of ESG factors across investment sectors and markets. In our view, understanding this dynamic is the key to successfully incorporating ESG risks and opportunities into portfolio construction.

For many investors, whether fixed income or equity, the process of integrating ESG factors into their strategies begins with correlating the relevance of each factor to individual industries. At a basic level this shows, for example, that greenhouse gas emissions are a particular risk for mining companies and electric utilities, while customer privacy is a key concern for the healthcare sector.

This is a good starting point but offers an incomplete perspective. We believe a much deeper dive is necessary to fully dimension the materiality of ESG factors for portfolio performance. Investors need to know how a particular factor may affect investment returns for a given sector or market.

Factors Can Have Wide or Narrow Impacts

Factor attribution using historical returns can reveal how ESG factors have contributed to investment returns in the past, whether for a sector or an entire investment universe, in equities or in bonds.

We’ve observed that some factors can be financially material for all companies in a market, regardless of sector. For example, we divided stocks in the MSCI All Country World Index into quintiles according to their total recordable incident rate (TRIR)—the number of workplace injuries or illnesses—then compared their returns relative to the parent index over 14 years (Display, left). The results show that high TRIR consistently underperformed the market and that low TRIR consistently outperformed.

Some ESG Factors Affect Entire Markets

Historical analysis does not guarantee future results.

TRIR: total recordable incident rate; social fines are defined as fines divided by company revenue; MSCI ACWI is equal weighted; indices are divided into five quintiles, with Q1 being the highest measure of the factor and Q5 being the lowest.

Through December 31, 2024

Source: Bloomberg, Good Jobs First, MSCI, company reports, government reports and AllianceBernstein (AB)

Similarly, in the bond market, “social fines” is a powerful, index-wide factor (Display, right). Social fines are regulatory penalties imposed for nonenvironmental reasons, such as workplace health and safety and anticompetitive practices.

Other ESG factors with broad relevance across investment sectors include CEOs’ length of tenure and employee turnover. For investors wishing to integrate ESG factors into their portfolios, it’s useful, in our view, to know which factors have index-wide applicability.

Factor attribution can also reveal which ESG factors are particularly relevant to a specific sector and which have historically shown no financial materiality (Display).

In Other Cases, Factor Relevance Varies by Sector

Water Usage Intensity vs. Annualized Return Relative to the MSCI All Country World Index (Percent)

Historical analysis does not guarantee future results.

Water usage intensity is defined as cubic meters of water consumed divided by revenue in US dollars; index sectors are equal weighted then divided into five quintiles, with Q1 being the highest water usage and Q5 being the lowest.

January 1, 2010, through December 31, 2024

Source: Company reports, MSCI and AB

Another advantage of factor attribution is that it can lead to observations that are unexpected and even counterintuitive. We found, for example, that companies with high ESG disclosures broadly performed better than those with low or no disclosures, regardless of whether their ESG practices were good, bad or indifferent. In the case of ESG metrics where there was no significant under- or overperformance relative to the market—CFO tenure and split roles for CEO and chair of the board—companies that disclosed data outperformed companies that didn’t disclose, on average.

Fundamental Research Enhances Insights from Factor Attribution

But factor attribution alone is not enough, in our view; it should complement fundamental research.

Understanding the effect of ESG factors on performance is most valuable in the context of broader research into how well a company is managed. For example, fundamental research can show that a high TRIR affects productivity directly, through lost working hours, and indirectly, by creating a culture in which workers are undermotivated because they don’t feel safe. Additionally, factor attribution works best with long data series, which are not always available, stressing the importance of fundamental research.

Another way fundamental research can help is in measuring ESG factors appropriately to a particular sector, instead of taking the generic approach typically used by many third-party ESG databases. This could mean, for example, measuring carbon emissions in terms of miles per gallon for automakers, per passenger mile for airliners and per ton of cement produced for building-material companies.

And it can tease out the nuances underlying many ESG factors. In the case of the mining sector, for example, fundamental research can focus on tailings dam risk within the more broadly defined factors of water and hazardous materials management (Display).

Deep and Complex Insights Can Be Captured Quickly and Efficiently

Example of an ESG Factor Map

For illustrative purposes only

As of April 30, 2025

Source: AB

As this small snapshot of an ESG materiality matrix shows, these insights can be mapped very simply. But it’s the quality of the information behind it that gives the map its value: the understanding of how ESG factors can be financially material across investment sectors, industries and markets. By embedding such knowledge in their securities research and portfolio construction, investors, in our view, may significantly enhance the potential for outperformance.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations and do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams. Views are subject to revision over time.

MSCI makes no express or implied warranties or representations, and shall have no liability whatsoever with respect to any MSCI data contained herein. The MSCI data may not be further redistributed or used as a basis for other indices or any securities or financial products. This report is not approved, reviewed or produced by MSCI.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: NET Power Looks More Attractive Now, Yet It Needs More Time (And Money)
data.description:
Thesis

A clean energy company attracting top-tier firms as investors and partners, while having the Rice Family, well known for its natural gas and clean energy investment projects, directly involved, would have been considered poised for success. And that was the case for NET Power (NPWR). However, early-stage projects years away from commercial production often face regulatory obstacles, delays, and increased capital needs. NET Power was no exception, and a negative FY 2024 ER resulted in a major stock sell-off. While uncertainty prevails and things seem to go south, NET Power's project remains compelling, investors and partners are still on board, and its valuation below its net-cash position is now more attractive than ever. Thus, this may be the right time to consider NET Power a more friendly investment. On the other hand, investors should acknowledge certain risks and be aware of the long-term perspective that comes with the possibility of failure.

A Brief Company Overview

NET Power was founded in 2010 and is headquartered in Durham, North Carolina. The company commenced trading on the NYSE on 06/09/2023 after completing the SPAC merger with Rice Acquisition Corp. II., which resulted in $675mm gross proceeds, and a ~$1.5B enterprise value. Danny Rice serves as CEO. NET Power attracted top-tier investors, including 8 Rivers (SK Group), Constellation Energy Corporation (SEG), Occidental Petroleum Corporation (OXY), and Baker Hughes Company (BKR).

The company is currently undergoing leadership restructuring. On 05/01/2025, Brian Allen, former President and COO, and Akash Patel, former CFO, were released from their duties. Danny Rice assumed the President and interim CFO role, and Marc Horstman, NET Power’s Head of Product Development, was appointed COO.

NET Power aspires to provide clean and reliable energy at a relatively low cost compared to other solutions. Natural gas fuels its proprietary "NET Power Cycle" system that produces electricity while capturing nearly all the CO2 produced in the process and not producing other air pollutants.

NET Power developed its demo facility at La Porte, Texas, to validate its technology. The La Porte facility commenced operations in 2018, has validated NET Power technologies to date, and will continue to do so in the future, to derisk the project. Its closest project to production is the Project Permian, in West Texas, expected online in 2029. There are also various other projects at early stages of development. The company ideally seeks to develop joint utility and industrial-scale projects at sites near shore and CO2 storage locations, with access to abundant, low-cost natural gas.

Latest Earnings/Business updates

FY 2024 ER and the stock sell-off

On 03/10/2025, NET Power announced its Q4 and FY 2024 ER. The announcement triggered a sell-off, bringing the stock over 50% lower in two days. The stock price haemorrhaging continued, and from ~$7 before the announcement, it now trades at ~$1.60 per share. The market showed pessimism regarding the financing, execution, and viability of the Permian and future projects. The nearly 100% increased cost estimate the CEO mentioned during the earnings call was enough to disappoint investors. Inflationary pressures and site-specific challenges changed the management's forecasts.

Q1 2025 ER

On 05/12/2025, the company announced its Q1 2025 ER and the progress made regarding its projects and financials. Cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments totalled ~$500mm on 03/31/2025 with zero debt. The CEO underlined the company's commitment to delivering cost reductions for the Project Permian. He also believes the company can achieve a competitive LCOE compared to peers. Tests at the La Porte facility will further enhance the project's commercialisation efforts and the turbo expander validation program with Baker Hughes. The company expects to spend ~$190mm in total for FY 2025, resulting in ~$350mm on 12/31/2025. The CEO also mentioned the new generation nuclear plants and estimated a much smaller cost for the Project Permian (expected to be the most expensive and less efficient NET Power project) compared to them.

The COO highlighted 3 key developments the company will be focused on in 2025.

  • Completion of phases 1&2 of the location-agnostic turbo expander validation program with Baker Hughes at the La Porte facility, as expected.
  • Optimising cost-saving efforts for Project Permian with partners to bring costs down and gain experience for future deployments.
  • Progressing on the feasibility study on a standardised modular multiunit plant.

Competition

NET Power doesn't have direct competitors as it develops its proprietary, patented technology. However, competition won't be absent because traditional and clean energy players will claim a market share. A more specified peer group could include the new generation of clean fossil-fuel-powered projects. An example that the CEO often mentions is the new generation of nuclear plants and the SMRs. NET Power's competitive advantage will be its reliable, 24/7 clean energy production, ideally at the lowest cost in the US market. It should also be much cheaper and faster to develop its projects, using an abundant fuel source. Its technology is getting derisked through its La Porte demonstration facility, and it remains to be seen if the leadership aspirations become a reality.

Valuation

NET Power is at an early, pre-revenue stage, and its first commercial deployment is expected in 2029 at best. Based on that fact and acknowledging the latest negative developments concerning the increased costs, the company will probably be valued near its net cash position (~$500mm as of 03/31/2025) unless favourable developments or a positive update occur. Its market cap is ~$350mm, representing its net cash position exiting 2025. Thus, there is a margin of safety regarding a significant reduction of the share price, however, the upward space is also limited for now. Getting closer to the completion of the Project Permian could provide better visibility for the next projects as well. Valuation could suddenly be boosted on good news, short-term, although a near net cash position valuation seems reasonable. It should also be underlined that the company was expected to need an additional $600-900mm to complete the Permian Project.

Risks

  • The company announced nearly 100% increased costs for Project Permian, raising uncertainty about its business model viability.
  • There may be delays in project development due to supply chain constraints.
  • Natural gas prices could present volatility.
  • Competitors may prove more efficient and claim a larger market share than expected.
  • Macro/market/geopolitical conditions can negatively impact NET Power.
  • There is still uncertainty regarding government policies (45Q, etc.).
  • Increased costs will probably result in debt issuance and/or shareholders' dilution sooner or later.
  • The stock price may face extreme volatility.

Final Thoughts

Summarising, NET Power presented a compelling project that was recently derailed mainly due to financing and execution matters. The top-tier investors and the Rice family experience, as well as the valuation near net cash and cash equivalents levels, provide some safety from a significant decrease in the stock price and confidence that the project will succeed, and despite increased costs, will find a way to claim an important market share. The project cannot be presumed dead as the NPWR market cap implies, however, there are certain risks. Investors who believe in the project should examine initiating a position, but not go all-in, acknowledging the risks. Alternatively, they could wait for more clarity regarding financing and execution. It's probably not the time to sell either, at such a poor valuation. In any case, a cautious approach is recommended.

data.title: Grupo Carso Sees Profitability Deteriorate With Less Government Infrastructure Work
data.description:
Grupo Carso (OTCPK:GPOVF) (OTCPK:GPOVY) released last month its 1Q25 results.

As commented in previous articles on the name, the level of reporting presented by GCarso for each segment is really scarce, considering the size of the conglomerate. However, by listening to the earnings call (half of which was in Spanish and not translated), we can learn more about the performance of important segments.

Overall, the situation is one of deterioration in two segments (retail via Sanborns and Construction), of stagnation in others (industrial, cement), and improvement in one (Energy and Gas Transport participations).

In terms of valuation, the name is now more expensive than a quarter ago, trading at a market cap of MXN 280 billion (~$14.5 billion), which is more than 18x FY24 earnings (and probably even more based on FY25 earnings). This seems like a high multiple for a company like GCarso, and therefore, I maintain a Hold rating.

1Q25 results by segment

As mentioned above, part of the information reported below can be found on the 1Q25 report (in English) and in the Q&A session of the earnings call (in Spanish). The comparisons to FY24 come from the 4Q24 report.

The bad

Sanborns keeps suffering: Sanborns is (was) one of the company's main segments. It is a large retail operation with more than 450 stores, including several brands (Sanborns, iShop, Sears, among others).

The segment is suffering from a retraction in consumer spending that is hitting its bottom line even more strongly because of operating leverage. The segment's EBITDA margin fell from 10% in 1Q23 to 8.4% in 1Q24 and now 6.7% in 1Q25.

During the call, management emphasized the need to drive higher store productivity, but without specific measures, for example, on capital allocation (store expansion or closures).

Construction lost clients: The Construction segment has lost an important revenue channel from government infrastructure projects. The segment lost 33% of revenues YoY and is now operating at breakeven EBITDA. The construction of a natural gas pipeline from the Energy segment is helping, but unless infrastructure picks up, this segment will probably generate breakeven EBITDA or a loss for the year.

The neutral

Industry is flat in real terms: The industrial segment (mainly the manufacturing of telecom cables) presented supposed growth of 20% in revenues at constant margins (11% operating). This was not generated by real volumes, but almost entirely by FX depreciation in Mexico (20% YoY). Given the segment exports a lot of its production, revenues and profits increase in line, but they are basically flat in USD.

Cement retrenching: Cement is in a similar position, growing 10% because of FX rates (exports to the US), but worse compared to industrial, it lost margins, only maintaining profitability in MXN terms (therefore losing in USD terms). This makes sense given the fall in construction in Mexico.

The good

GCarso has three segments dedicated to Energy and O&G which are the current outperforming businesses.

Gas transportation: One of those segments is Energy proper, which participates in natural gas transportation. It owns 100% of one pipeline (Samalayuca-Sasabe), which is consolidated and grew EBITDA 25% YoY (above MXN depreciation). It is building another one (Centauro del Norte), which should be operative in 2026.

GCarso also owns 50% of two pipelines connecting Texas with Mexico (Waha-Presidio and Waha-San Elisario), which have performed similarly to Samalayuca, but whose results are not consolidated into the company's operating income.

Oil E&P: The company's last segment is Zamajal, which owns participations in two oil projects. One is the Petrobal, already in the exploitation stage, on the coast of the south of Mexico. The segment is currently losing money, but only because it is investing in expanding production. However, according to management, investment and new income from higher production should balance out by year-end 2025.

Finally, GCarso announced the purchase of a majority stake in Talos Mexico in 2024, which owns a 17% participation in a Pemex-operated field on the coast of Veracruz called Zama. The acquisition has not yet been approved by the regulatory authorities, and therefore, operations are not consolidated. Further, exploitation did not start in Zama, and Pemex is considering offering the operations to a private player, further delaying the start.

Valuation remains unattractive

In FY24, GCarso generated a net profit of MXN 15 billion, of which commercial, construction, and cement represented 20% of profits each, and industrial another 33%. The energy segments, although growing, are much smaller.

From the comments above, we can expect commercial and construction to do worse this year, with industrial and cement potentially maintaining profitability, but also potentially facing problems if the economy in Mexico or the US deteriorates.

Compared to this, GCarso currently trades at about MXN 280 billion, which is 18x FY24 earnings, and potentially even more in FY25 earnings. Considering the company's qualitative aspects mentioned in the original article on the name (lack of long-term growth, lack of detailed reporting, conglomerate discount), and the generally more negative than neutral read on the Mexican and US economies, I believe that a 18x earnings multiple is high for GCarso.

For that reason, I maintain a Hold rating on the name.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Tesla's Robotaxi Revolution Begins In June - Growth May Already Be Priced In
data.description:
Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) is gearing up to begin testing its robotaxi program in Austin, Texas, beginning in June 2025. With 10 Model Y vehicles being dedicated to unsupervised, full self-driving [FSD] to begin field-testing the program, this could be a major paradigm shift for Tesla as it ramps up the program in 2026, potentially leading to an uplift in growth potential.

Despite the optimistic outlook for Tesla, I believe TSLA shares are priced appropriately to my growth forecast. For this reason, I am downgrading TSLA shares to a HOLD rating with a price target of $329/share at 54.39x eFY26 EV/aEBITDA.

Tesla Operations

Tesla remains on track to begin its robotaxi program in Austin, Texas, by the end of June 2025. Accordingly, Tesla is planning to begin the program with 10 Tesla vehicles before scaling out to ensure its success. Specific to the Cybercab, Tesla will begin equipment installation at its Gigafactory Austin, Texas facility in Q2'25 and is expecting to begin production in 2026.

To ensure the program is effective, Tesla will employ a team to remotely monitor the vehicles. In his interview with CNBC, Elon Musk indicated that the robotaxi program will begin with Model Y vehicles with FSD Unsupervised enabled. Though the program isn't expected to be accretive to the bottom line initially, Musk noted in the q1'25 earnings call that the program may begin generating income by e2'26.

Tesla may have a significant advantage over competitor Waymo, Alphabet's (GOOG) take on the robotaxi. Waymo uses an assortment of LiDAR, cameras, and radar to create a 3D map of its surroundings and renders the information through its AI software. This compares to Tesla leveraging cameras and AI software. Looking at Alphabet's q1'25 results, Waymo commenced paid services in Silicon Valley and expanded into Austin. In addition to this, Waymo is expected to expand into Atlanta in mid-2025 and reach Washington, D.C. and Miami in 2026.

In q4'24, General Motors (GM) fully absorbed Cruise and sunset its robotaxi program, refocusing efforts towards FSD services through its Super Cruise program.

Musk remains optimistic about the Optimus humanoid robot rollout. Accordingly, Musk anticipates that Tesla will have the capacity to produce 1mm units per year in 5-years' time. The use cases for such a robot can be vast, including industrial and manufacturing. Given the robot's dexterity, it can be expected that Optimus could potentially be interchanged with human labor in more hazardous environments, such as a steelmaking facility, chemicals, plant, or a disaster zone. For example, an Optimus robot could potentially execute a search-and-rescue mission following a tornado in the Midwest or a hurricane along the Gulf Coast, minimizing the risks of injury.

I'm somewhat skeptical of Optimus's role in manufacturing given the vast capabilities of purpose-built autonomous factories, designed by companies like Rockwell Automation (ROK), Emerson Electric (EMR), or Symbotic (SYM).

Granted, the Optimus robot may provide significantly more functionality when compared to a robotic arm; however, this level of functionality may not be necessary for most industrial environments. For example, I would expect that an Optimus, or a fleet of Optimus, can functionally build a house more effectively than a robotic arm. The question is, will Optimus be used in these types of environments and will this level of functionality be necessary. Sticking with the homebuilding example, I believe Optimus could potentially augment the construction and manufacturing labor force. My take is that Optimus may not need a "clean" environment to function, meaning that a factory may not need to be retrofitted for the humanoid robot to effectively augment workers. I'd suspect that the humanoid robot can climb stairs or a ladder and maneuver uneven surfaces much more effectively than a robot on wheels.

The industrial robotics market is expected to grow at a 12.6% CAGR from 2025-2030, potentially providing a strong market for Tesla to enter into. I suspect the market may realize support from industrial reshoring or migration resulting from the shifting international trade policies.

Vehicle production & deliveries each experienced significant declines in q1'25 as Tesla shifted Model Y manufacturing facilities to build the updated model. Despite this transition, "Other Models" realized a sharp -24% year-over-year decline in the quarter. Total net vehicles in the quarter resulted in a sequential inventory build.

S&P Global is estimating that global vehicle sales will grow by 1.7% in 2025 to 89.6mm units. In addition to this, the share of battery-electric vehicle sales is expected to grow across all regions, potentially exhibiting a strong market for Tesla's vehicle fleet.

I am cautious when considering these estimates given the macroeconomic headwinds consumers face. Though the consumer outlook remains relatively positive, discretionary spending may slow, favoring essentials.

In the near-term, Tesla's energy business is exposed to tariffs given the prominence of battery manufacturing in China. To mitigate tariff risks, Tesla is working to localize more manufacturing; however, developing these resources will take some time, providing no immediate relief. Management noted in the q1'25 earnings call that the high-volume vehicle programs in North America sources 85% of content from North America and 95% of Shanghai is sourced locally.

Accordingly, Tesla commenced operations at its Texas lithium refining factory at the end of 2024, bringing more of the battery manufacturing supply chain in-house. The facility is expected to have 50GWh in lithium refining capacity, allowing Tesla to potentially offset certain costs from imported resources.

Tesla Financial Position

Tesla faced certain headwinds exiting q1'25, particularly in vehicle sales. Despite manufacturing changeover for Model Y occurring, vehicle production in the quarter outweighed deliveries by nearly 26k units.

Looking to eq2'25, I'm forecasting Tesla to generate just shy of $25b in net revenue, with an adjusted EPS of $0.50/share. Given the general sentiment towards Tesla vehicles right now, as cited in my previous coverage going into q1'25, I suspect vehicle sales may remain relatively challenged for the time being. Despite this factor, I believe Tesla may realize support from its growing power and services segments. I expect services to continue to expand as the total addressable market, meaning Tesla models on the road, continues to increase.

Risks Related to Tesla

Bull Case

Tesla remains on track to begin its robotaxi program in Austin, Texas, in June 2025. This program may be a major paradigm shift for Tesla in the coming years if the organization can successfully scale production of its Cybercabs and effectively manage the program.

Though the case for Optimus may be relatively narrow off-the-cuff, I suspect there may be significant value to extract from the program given the robots' unique capabilities when compared to the less versatile industrial robotics products on the market. The humanoid robots could potentially augment human capital without the need to retrofit facilities, posing a major value to organizations that may seek to minimize downtime while enhancing their productivity.

Bear Case

Given the negative public sentiment towards Tesla vehicles as part of Musk's participation with the Department of Government Efficiency [DOGE], the robotaxi program may be a risky endeavor given that the vehicles will be unsupervised on the road. Though I do not expect it to impact the program, there is the potential for vandalism and theft of the vehicles while on the road, potentially hindering the test phase, pushing out the mass adoption phase.

Industrial robotics may be a narrower market than anticipated, given the purpose-built robots on the market today. In addition to this, industrial automation by companies like Rockwell Automation may be favored over humanoid robots given their purpose-built approach to automating processes.

Valuation and Shareholder Value

TSLA shares are currently trading at the higher end of their historical range at 75.53x EV/aEBITDA on a trailing twelve-month basis. Given the high premium and my financial forecast, I believe growth is priced into shares. For this reason, I am downgrading TSLA shares to a HOLD rating with a price target of $329/share at 54.39x eFY26 EV/aEBITDA.

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm's "financial position" section and ties it to the stock's historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle, with the blue-sky scenario being the stock's peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company's stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market cap and relative multiple.

data.title: Costamare Bulkers: Interesting Opportunity, But Management Has To Perform For Long-Term Investment
data.description:
Investment thesis

Costamare Bulkers Holdings Limited (NYSE:CMDB) is experiencing selling pressure due to Costamare (CMRE) shareholders not interested in their drybulk adventure. This has caused the stock to drop to one third of its NAV while the cheapest drybulk stocks trade around half NAV. If the stock re-rates to half its NAV, it will trade at $14.7 per share, representing around a 50% upside.

Background

Costamare Bulkers Holdings Limited is a drybulk company with two different divisions, the owned vessels and the CBI platform. CBI is an operating platform with chartered in/out vessels that can take directional and relative positions on drybulk freight rates.

The company is a drybulk spin-off of Costamare Inc., which started to acquire drybulk vessels in 2021 with the huge profits from the container boom due to COVID. The Konstantakopoulos family, the sponsor, maintains a 63% ownership of the new company.

While the acquisition timing was great at the start of a rising market, results have been poor with losses over the last two years despite bringing top-notch management from the competition.

When the drybulk segment was inside Costamare, it was difficult to assess the exact performance. With the spin-off will be much easier to compare the results with peers.

As of March 2025, the company owns 38 drybulk vessels and has 48 chartered-in through the CBI platform. Most owned vessels are on linked index charters; however, since the beginning of 2025 they have been converting some index-linked charters to fixed rates.

Theoretically, the CBI platform should allow for greater flexibility to optimize fleet positioning, profit from dislocations and enhance profitability overall; however, until now, it has not been clear whether it has helped profitability. Future earnings and better disclosure will be key to analyse its results.

Regarding the capital allocation, there are two priorities. 1) Growth and 2) Returning capital to shareholders.

Growth seems to be focused on the CBI platform, since the company made the last vessel acquisition in Q1 2022 and has been selling some ships. If CBI manages to achieve profitability, it could boost profits with little capital invested.

It seems that returning capital to shareholders will mainly be through dividends and related to operating cash flow. Given the low leverage and financial strength, it would be better to have a determined dividend policy, but dividends will be subject to Board of Directors discretion. Costamare did repurchase in the past; however, it doesn’t seem a priority despite the huge discount to NAV.

Financial Position and Valuation

Costamare Bulkers has a very strong financial position, being almost net cash.

With the spin-off completion, the company will have $160 million in cash and just $172 million in debt while owning 38 vessels. The adjusted values of cash and debt correspond to a $100 million cash contribution, the cancellation of $85 million in related party loans, and $164 million in debt repayment from Costamare Inc. before the spin-off.

The company provided a NAV estimate; however, I have made some adjustments to be on the conservative side. First, in this slide the company states $205 million in cash, while in the previous slide it was $160 million. I will use $160 million. I will use Arctic asset values to determine the fleet value.

With those adjustments, NAV stands at $710 million, slightly lower than the company estimate, and represents a $29.3 NAV per share. This valuation assumes 0 value to the CBI platform.

The stock started trading at $13.5 per share, but it rapidly moved down and currently trades at $10 per share, at one third of NAV. There could still be some selling pressure as legacy shareholders sell the stock, but the stock price seems to be stabilizing.

There are 24.2 million shares outstanding and the Konstantakopoulos family owns around 15.5 million of them, meaning a float of around 9 million. The volume is around 200k shares per trading day, so it could take a while until the Costamare legacy shareholders that want to sell exit their positions.

However, once this selling pressure finishes, the stock could see a re-rating as the worst valued peers are trading at 0.5 times NAV. Assuming the stock trades at such a multiple, the fair value should be $14.7 per share, representing almost a 50% upside potential. Such valuation could improve if the management demonstrates that they are good operators and CBI turns out to be profitable on a consistent basis.

I have initiated a small tracking position until there is more clear evidence that selling pressure subsides.

Risks

There are several risks, partially mitigated due to the low valuation.

Costamare dry bulk operations have been loss making for the last two years, underperforming peers. If this situation continues, it will affect the stock valuation.

There could still be substantial selling pressure, and given the low volumes, it could cause the stock to drop for several weeks.

Finally, the controlling shareholder is probably not willing to sell the company, even with a sustained low valuation. This will make the company almost impossible for an acquisition.

Conclusion

Costamare Bulkers has to demonstrate that it can be profitable like its peers. In that scenario, there is significant price appreciation potential.

The company trades at one third its NAV, while the lowest valued peers trade at half of its NAV. On the other hand, the companies with the best management and operations like SBLK or GOGL trade higher. If the company demonstrates good results, it could trade with a smaller discount.

However, the stock could still face some selling pressure due to legacy Costamare shareholders not interested in the dry bulk sector.

data.title: Welcome To The End Of Tasks
data.description:
By Christopher Gannatti, CFA

We've crossed an invisible boundary. The core story is no longer about intelligence that mimics language, but about systems that act. That distinction matters—immensely. Because the leap from models to agents is not linear progress. It's a paradigm shift.

From Passive Models to Active Agents

In 2025, AI crossed a subtle but monumental threshold: it stopped just answering questions and started executing goals. This transition—from models to agents—isn't about better search results. It's about capability. The latest models, like OpenAI's o1, show stark gains on benchmarks that test not just knowledge but reasoning and follow-through. Take AIME, the American Invitational Mathematics Examination—an infamously difficult math contest that pushes problem-solving across multiple steps. GPT-4o scored just 9.3%. o1? Its score jumped to 74.4%. That's not a tweak. It's a redefinition of what AI can handle.

Other benchmarks tell a similar story. On MATH, a test derived from high school and early college competition problems, o1-preview achieved 94.8% accuracy. On GPQA Diamond, a collection of tough, graduate-level multiple-choice questions that require nuanced reasoning, o1 pushed past 77%, versus GPT-4o's 50.6%. These are proxies for tasks that require patience, logic and an understanding of context. Not just "what's the capital of France?" but "solve this, adjust that, check your work." And in those domains, agents are starting to outperform not only older models, but skilled humans on short timeframes.

The economic and workflow implications are profound. Agentic AI isn't just smarter, it's beginning to work the way people do: planning, executing, refining. It's slower and more expensive, yes—o1 runs at 40 times the latency and costs multiples more per token—but that's what you'd expect from a high-level specialist. We're entering a world where the fast models handle everyday questions, and the slow agents tackle the hard stuff. These systems won't just change software—they'll change who does the work, how it's done, and what we even mean by productivity.

Figure 1: Measuring How Models Are Gaining Different, More Agentic Capabilities

In investing, we're trained to pay attention not just to what performs, but how and under what conditions it performs. That same mindset is now essential in evaluating AI. While most benchmarks measure how well a model answers trivia or solves math problems in isolation, the new HELMET benchmark (results shown in figure 2) shifts the focus to something far more consequential: can a model operate across sprawling, high-complexity contexts, the way an analyst does when synthesizing insights from 200 pages of earnings transcripts? This is the benchmark for models transitioning from tools to agents—capable not just of task completion, but of orchestration, reasoning and memory over time.

The results are revealing. Older models like GPT-4 and Claude 3.5 Sonnet see their performance drop sharply as context lengths stretch—like interns who ace short memos but stumble when handed the full quarterly report. But models like GPT-4o and Gemini 1.5 Pro show durability: they hold their ground even at 128,000 tokens (think reading and recalling War and Peace in one go). These models didn't just get smarter—they became better long-term thinkers. That's the hidden differentiator in an enterprise context, where agents won't just summarize a page—they'll navigate whole data rooms, legal filings and CRM logs to inform decisions.

For investors, HELMET isn't just another benchmark—it's a glimpse into what makes a model truly enterprise-grade. In the same way that we evaluate businesses not on one good quarter but on resilience through cycles, HELMET exposes which models bend and which break under pressure. And as agentic AI starts to augment—or even automate—complex workflows across finance, legal and healthcare, understanding these capabilities will matter as much as knowing the difference between growth and value. The models that remember, reason and react over time are building something bigger than intelligence—they're building leverage.

Figure 2: HELMET—How Robust Are Models across Lengthening Context Windows?

There's a chart in the 2025 AI Index Report that quietly rewrites our expectations for artificial intelligence. It tracks how well different AI systems perform relative to a reasoning benchmark, RE-Bench (shown in figure 3), as they're given more time and multiple attempts. At first, humans outperform the machines. But something remarkable happens as the time budget increases: agentic AI systems—those designed to iterate, self-correct and plan—begin to pull ahead. By the 32-hour mark, the best agents are outperforming humans, not because they're inherently smarter, but because they're designed to compound intelligence over time.

This is the difference between a clever tool and a self-improving system. Static models, no matter how large, hit plateaus. But wrap the same model in an agentic framework like AIDE and performance scales with patience. The story here isn't just technological; it's deeply financial. The best AI agents look less like flash-in-the-pan geniuses and more like investors with a repeatable process—learning from every mistake, reinvesting every insight. They aren't just answering questions; they're conducting experiments with feedback loops that mimic the logic of compounding returns.

For financial professionals, this is more than a benchmark result—it's a signal. We're approaching a world where autonomous agents can outperform human teams in certain domains, given enough time and computing power. The takeaway is simple: the edge won't go to the biggest model, or even the smartest—it will go to the one that learns best, longest. Just like in markets, time and iteration are the ultimate advantages. Investors who grasp this shift early will be better positioned to understand which AI platforms, infrastructure providers and application layers will drive the next wave of productivity and enterprise value.

Figure 3: RE-Bench Results

The Age of Agency Has Arrived

What RE-Bench, HELMET and real-world deployments all suggest is that we've crossed into a fundamentally different computing paradigm—one where intelligence isn't measured by output per prompt, but by compounding returns on cognition. The hallmark of this shift isn't higher accuracy in a vacuum, but the emergence of feedback-driven systems that can plan, adapt and persist over time. Like a seasoned analyst or portfolio manager, agentic AI doesn't just seek the right answer—it develops a process to keep finding better ones. And that process, once embedded into workflows, creates leverage that traditional software—and static models—simply can't match.

From Leverage to Alpha

For investors, the implication is clear: the next frontier of productivity won't be about who has the largest model, but who can most effectively integrate agentic systems into their value chains. Expect the winners to be those who shift their mental models—from tools that assist to systems that compound. That means looking beyond parameter counts or one-off benchmarks, and instead asking: Can this system learn over time? Can it operate autonomously across messy, long-horizon domains? Can it create feedback loops that scale? Those are the questions that will separate hype from durable alpha. Because the real opportunity here isn't automation—it's transformation.

Important Information Related to this Article

Source for all data in this blog post, including figures: Maslej et al., The AI Index 2025 Annual Report, AI Index Steering Committee, Institute for Human-Centered AI, Stanford University, 4/25.

Christopher Gannatti, CFA, Global Head of Research

Christopher Gannatti began at WisdomTree as a Research Analyst in December 2010, working directly with Jeremy Schwartz, CFA®, Director of Research. In January of 2014, he was promoted to Associate Director of Research where he was responsible to lead different groups of analysts and strategists within the broader Research team at WisdomTree. In February of 2018, Christopher was promoted to Head of Research, Europe, where he was based out of WisdomTree’s London office and was responsible for the full WisdomTree research effort within the European market, as well as supporting the UCITs platform globally. In November 2021, Christopher was promoted to Global Head of Research, now responsible for numerous communications on investment strategy globally, particularly in the thematic equity space. Christopher came to WisdomTree from Lord Abbett, where he worked for four and a half years as a Regional Consultant. He received his MBA in Quantitative Finance, Accounting, and Economics from NYU’s Stern School of Business in 2010, and he received his bachelor’s degree from Colgate University in Economics in 2006. Christopher is a holder of the Chartered Financial Analyst Designation.

Original Post

data.title: FMAG ETF: Strong Bounceback From Liberation Day; Upgrade To Hold
data.description:
What is FMAG?

The Fidelity Magellan ETF (FMAG) is an actively managed exchange-traded fund (also known as ETF) that invests in mostly large-cap American listed stocks, with additional minor exposure (under 5%) in global equities outside of the U.S. Fidelity is pitching that they have the potential to outperform the broad market S&P 500 index, using fundamental analysis of factors such as each issuer’s financial condition and industry position, as well as market and economic conditions, to select investments that outperform the benchmark. I recently wrote about FMAG's historical underperformance, but have been surprised by the fund's bounceback after Liberation Day. After reviewing the fund facts and quarterly fund commentary from management, I'm improving the rating to hold, as it seems like this has lit a fire under the fund.

FMAG's benchmark target is the S&P 500, with the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) and being an easy comparison - it's also an ETF, but is passively managed. That means it's fund managers don't make many trades, and only rebalance every once in a while, versus Fidelity's managers, who have the mandate to buy and sell stocks more often if they feel that there's a compelling investment opportunity.

After analyzing the price movement of the past few months, I've been impressed to see FMAG's managers make some good bets within the fund. While the fund has not materially beat its benchmark, while also presenting more volatility during selloffs, at higher fees, I am amending my rating to hold to wait and see if they can navigate this period of uncertainty better than the passive index. That said, I still recommend investors buy SPY or VOO if they want to get exposure to U.S. equities at a low fee.

Fund Breakdown

FMAG was launched on Feb. 2, 2021 by Fidelity with a mandate to create a diversified domestic equity strategy with a large-cap growth profile in a fund that seeks capital appreciation. Fidelity also noted to investors that they plan "to identify high-quality growth stocks benefiting from long-term "mega trends," as well as the three "B's" – brands, barriers to entry and "best in class" management teams – using a proprietary quantitative screen and bottom-up fundamental analysis". Ultimately, Fidelity was basically launching a large-cap fund to track, and hopefully beat, the S&P 500, and be more actively managed to generate additional alpha from those returns, using unique risk-managed tools for their asset managers. The fund also had 62% turnover over the past three months, which is appropriate.

In the recent quarterly fund review, the fund actually underperformed to Mar. 31, due to significant underweighting in healthcare and energy, along with a large stake in Broadcom Inc (AVGO) which had slid 28%. But when others, including myself, were skeptical, the fund delivered strong returns thereafter. The underweighting in healthcare meant the fund averted United Health Group Inc's (UNH) collapse in April, and oil's collapse on tariff worries as well. Additionally, AVGO roared back with a $10Bn buyback and rode the tech wave, leading to FMAG outperforming on multiple fronts over the past several weeks. Overall, the fund managers stuck to their guns and have put together a nice response amidst the recent uncertainty.

The fund still employs a 0.6% yearly ETF expense ratio for the fees to the asset manager, which is several times higher than passive peers and they have underperformed in the past. For context, SPY's expense ratio fee is 0.09%, over 6 times smaller than FMAG, but seeing this response, FMAG certainly has built some credibility.

Previous Results & Fund Portfolio

When comparing FMAG's portfolio, there are some differences in the portfolio breakdown from SPY. The fund hasn't posted a recent fund facts after liberation day, but as of Mar. 31, the portfolio was heavily in top holdings like NVDA, MSFT and AMZN and sector diversification with double industrials exposure, potentially positioning well ahead of tariff reductions. The only large geographic difference includes some exposure to Ireland, Canada, Sweden and Uruguay. Ultimately, investors could still make investments in a couple of stocks and simply hold SPY if they want to outperform without paying a 0.6% fee yearly, but if they continue to outperform, it may be worth an investment.

From a risk perspective, a beta of 1.08 notes that the fund will have more volatility than the benchmark - and that has borne out in past results. Even though the prospectus by Fidelity noted that a risk-adjusted perspective would be used to generate alpha, the down years were down quite a bit more than the benchmark - and that's noted in the Sharpe ratio of 0.26. The Sharpe ratio measures risk-adjusted return, helping investors determine if they are being fairly compensated for the level of risk they are taking. The formula is the portfolio return minus the risk-free return, divided by the standard deviation. Essentially, it tracks the return required by investors for the risk they are taking, and it should be above 1 to indicate acceptable returns for the risk taken - the higher, the better.

Given that 0.26 is close to 0, it indicates that the fund is not really generating excess return relative to its risk. Even with the recent good news, there are still dozens of other offerings with lower expense and better Sharpe ratios that track SPY closely. Heck, even Fidelity has other funds that track the same benchmark that would make more sense, like FLCEX.

Risks & Conclusion

There are risks of concentration with this fund - they only invest in a basket of 51 equities, and have historically underperformed. But the fund has roared back since liberation day by avoiding bad apples in the large cap space, while making good tech bets. That said, I don't know if I can justify paying a 0.59% expense ratio compared to 0.09% for SPY or 0.03% for the Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO) which also has a proven risk-adjusted return profile and much more liquidity. This ETF has limited differentiation, doesn't trade much (daily volume of ~70,000 shares traded a day), has a low Sharpe ratio, and has a higher beta than its benchmark. FMAG has had a good short-term stint, and is worth keeping on your radar if this can be sustained.

data.title: PAAA: Among The Safest AAA CLO ETF
data.description:
Introduction

In my most recent coverage of AAA CLO funds, which I often espouse as a great place to allocate "risk-on" cash, or cash holdings that one is alright with having a bit of wiggle room (as opposed to savings accounts, CDs, or money markets that cannot fluctuate in price), I discussed that there is a safer alternative to most AAA CLO funds, which is the PGIM AAA CLO ETF (PAAA).

This is due for several reasons, which I'll cover in later sections of this article, but the most obvious way I can show this is how PAAA compared to the sector leader JAAA through the last market downturn and rebound in April.

What is an AAA CLO?

If you're new to AAA CLO funds like PAAA, this section is fairly important. Feel free to skip it if you're already "in the trenches" (or should I say "tranches"?) regarding CLOs.

This comes from a previous article on JAAA, since JAAA and PAAA hold the same kind of assets, AAA-rated collateralized loan obligations.

CLOs are [investment] vehicles that bundle loans together to create diversified baskets of risky assets, thus reducing the risk. These risky loans are typically senior secured debt, meaning they are backed by assets of some kind, by companies that [typically use them for leveraged buyouts]. It's not that the CLOs are risky within the structure; they get paid out first, especially AAA investors. It's that the companies that issue the loans CLOs buy up are inherently risky businesses by the virtue of them needing to issue floating rate debt. It is typically less preferable than fixed rate debt, at least in our past decade of low rates...

A big positive for CLO investors is that they are paid out first in any event of default, since the loans they pull from are at the top of the capital structure.

Here are the tranches of the CLO market and their 1993-2023 default rates. Note that there has never been a default of a CLO in the AAA or AA tranches since the inception of CLOs 30 years ago.

...

Compare CLOs 12-month drawdowns from 2012-2024, and we see a remarkably lower volatility asset than most debt instruments.

What Makes PAAA Different?

The difference comes within the AAA tranche itself, where CLOs are separated into "senior" and "junior," labels. While JAAA holds a mix of these assets, PAAA primarily invests in the senior tranche of AAA CLOs. This sets the fund apart, as it is the only CLO fund (to my knowledge) that specifically avoids the junior AAA tranche.

Looking at the makeup of PAAA against other leading CLO ETFs shows us how reliant many of these funds are on lower-rated CLOs, and how little they hold senior AAA CLOs compared to the index, which serves as a benchmark for essentially all AAA CLO ETFs.

In drawdowns, the difference between how senior and junior AAA tranches becomes even clearer. Junior AAA tranches tend to behave more like AA and A tranches during extreme market conditions, like in 2020, which is when those 1-week returns were clocked, I believe.

On the flip side, the safer the asset, the less it yields. Yields in CLOs are often a direct response to the risk they come with. Investing primarily in the senior AAA tranche means PAAA has an average yield less than its peers, which are loaded up on junior AAA, AA, and A tranches in their holdings.

This is classic risk/reward-based market behavior, and shouldn't necessarily be surprising to most folks.

There are, however, a group of folks that will be happy to accept a lower yield, especially since the yield is so close to peers, in order to have the lower drawdowns during stressful markets.

PAAA Holdings

Investing in CLO funds can be difficult because of the opaqueness of the vehicles themselves, as it's difficult to parse what is what once it's all been blended into "CLO soup," so to speak. This is why looking at the fund's holdings isn't particularly helpful in identifying risk. The names on the holdings are of the managers that packaged the CLOs, which is not indicative of what's inside those vehicles.

Here is PAAA's top ten:

One has to trust management to some large extent, as these CLOs are not easy to understand by the layperson, as these funds present them. This is a risk to investors present in all CLO funds, not just PAAA, as there are no passively run CLO funds and the index is not investable.

PAAA Dividends & Yield

Earlier, it was mentioned that PAAA carries a lower yield than some of its peers. This is true, although its yield is still fairly competitive, especially for its promise of lower volatility during market stress.

Keep in mind that YCharts uses trailing-twelve month ("TTM") yields.

These yields are falling primarily because of the falling Fed Funds Rate, which dictates short-term rates. These rates inform floating-rate debt like CLOs, which are typically the Secured Overnight Financing Rate ("SOFR") plus the prevailing credit spread for the assets in the CLO.

The Fed has signaled that they intend to hold rates for a while, and may only give one cut this year, which means that current rates may stay where they are for at least the Summer, if not longer. Investors should watch these closely, as they dictate CLO rates directly. The lower the Fed Funds Rate, the lower SOFR is, which in turn lowers PAAA's yield.

The dividends themselves are almost entirely income, with a bit of capital gains mixed in at the beginning of the year. The distributions are uneven, but don't vary too much month-to-month.

On a 30-day basis, the rate is currently 5.51%; this difference in yield is primarily due to how it is calculated, based on just the last thirty days instead of the last twelve months like the chart before.

Manager Commentary

The spreads on CLOs have been undergoing some turbulence recently, as noted by management, and shown to investors in the price volatility of PAAA over the last month.

PGIM remarked in their quarterly commentary on the CLO market:

The strong technical demand that supported collateralized loan obligations (CLOs) spreads through most of Q1 ultimately gave way toward the end of the period to softening collateral fundamentals and rising macro uncertainty. Spreads ended the period mixed across the CLO capital structure, with AAAs tightening around 1 bp to approximately 124 bps over three-month SOFR, while BBB spreads were roughly 35 bps wider at 305 bps over three-month SOFR.

We remain cognizant of potential credit risks in the underlying bank loans, which could outweigh prevailing technical support for CLOs. Further, while bank loan spreads have been supported by low net supply and strong demand, we expect to see some credit deterioration in the underlying CLO collateral via downgrades to CCC, increased default rates, and lower recovery rates. Indeed, although the loan default rate ended February at around 3.90%— down from 4.50% in January—we believe the prevailing high interest rate environment leaves room for defaults over the short term.

Ending the quarter, the PGIM AAA CLO ETF was the best performing ETF in its cohort. The Fund is invested entirely in AAA-rated bonds. In its AAA allocation, PGIM AAA CLO ETF has a senior AAA concentration of approximately 99.34% (relative to the benchmark’s approximately 96.55% of senior AAAs). As of 3/31/2025, the PGIM AAA CLO ETF had a nominal spread (carry) of approximately 3M SOFR+134 bps, compared to the carry of the index of approximately 3M SOFR+143 bps.

Seeing that PAAA was the best performing ETF in the category isn't surprising, especially given all the commentary thus far about senior vs. junior AAA tranches. It is definitively earned itself the title of "safest AAA CLO ETF."

Suitability

AAA CLO ETFs in general are suitable for all investors. These are some of the safest above-5% yields investors can access currently, and investors would likely be within their risk tolerance holding any amount of AAA CLO funds.

PAAA in particular is most suitable for conservative investors who are put off by the drawdowns JAAA saw in April, and prefer the safety of the senior AAA tranche. Investors who are willing to take on the additional risk to ratchet the yield up slightly may still prefer JAAA.

For very conservative investors, and for those that are intended to hold these positions as "dry powder," or cash ready to deploy for future investment opportunities, may prefer T-Bills ETFs that experience almost no drawdowns in adverse market conditions, where they tend to outperform the entire AAA CLO asset class. My recommendation for that category is SGOV.

With the caveat of the fund's 1-2% drawdown in adverse market conditions, this fund is as good as cash most of the time.

Conclusion

Ultimately, investors who are wary of AAA CLO ETFs like JAAA due to their recent volatility should seriously consider the PGIM AAA CLO ETF. The fund is invested in the highest credit rating CLOs, and distinguishes even within that category, aiming to be a safer, less volatile ride while still maintaining competitive yields.

It has earned the title of being the "safest AAA CLO ETF," due to its performance over the last month, where markets roiled, and AAA CLO ETFs broke across the board. PAAA outperformed during this time.

While I do not personally own PAAA, as I am okay with the risks that JAAA brings in excess of what PAAA does, there are plenty of folks for whom PAAA is the correct answer to which ETF they should choose from the pack. All AAA CLO ETFs are different, due to the need for active management in the area, and PAAA stands out.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: BILL FY Q3 Earnings: Take Rates Recover While Macro Weakness Hits - Buying Opportunity For The Long-Term
data.description:
• Core revenue broadly in-line, EPS beat. FY4Q and FY 2025 guidance cut.
  • AP/AR and Spend & Expense take rates rebound, calming fears stoked previous quarter.
  • Compelling valuation, with stock trading at 3x and 2.6x FY26 and FY27 core revenue estimates, and roughly 14x and 11.5 FY26 and FY27 FCF estimates.

Investment Thesis

BILL Holdings (NYSE:BILL) is a category leading Accounts Payable and financial operations automation cloud SaaS company in the SMB market. Using the BILL platform allows customers to get rid of legacy manual solutions and processes such as paper checks, reconciliations, paper invoice and bill management, etc. We believe BILL is an attractive long-term investment for the following reasons. 1) on our already lower than consensus figures, BILL is trading at 2.6x FY2027 core revenue (half of FY2027 is in calendar 2026), and at 11.5x FY2027 adjusted FCF multiple, which is attractive for a strong moat, high margin, category leading SaaS business such as BILL; 2) Building on its vast customer base, BILL is focused on cross-selling financing solutions such as invoice financing, or Spend & Expense service whereby it offers spend management platform allowing for real-time visibility on spending with corporate cards, expense reimbursement, and more. 3) BILL started developing AI agents, capitalizing on its large, invaluable data asset powered by detailed customer financial data collected on its platform.

FY3Q 2025 Performance

BILL reported FY3Q (calendar 1Q) earnings beating analyst estimates on both revenue, and adjusted EPS, however core revenue came in essentially in line. The revenue beat was mostly driven by float revenue, which is the interest BILL earns on the payment volume that goes through its system. Specifically, revenue came in at $358MM versus the guidance midpoint of $355 and analyst consensus of roughly $356MM. Core revenue was largely in line with $320MM, versus the guidance midpoint of $320MM and consensus of roughly $321MM. Adjusted EPS came in $0.5 per share, stronger than guidance and consensus, specifically guidance mid-point of $0.36 and the consensus estimate of $0.37. See the table below for details.

Despite the slight beat, the stock traded down roughly 6% after earnings. We must admit that this is a rather small move for this stock (one must simply look at price history), particularly given the guidance downgrade, however we think the reason is that it is already at such a low level that it prices-in significant bad news. The reason investors were underwhelmed by the earnings is because the company cut guidance for the year, with all of the cut falling on FY4Q estimates. Specifically, according to earlier guidance issued in February of this year, implied FY4Q core revenue was estimated at $350MM and adjusted EPS at $0.56 per share. The reduced guidance on May 8th now implies core revenue of $340MM, a reduction of $10MM, and adjusted EPS of $0.41 per share. The reduction in guidance was due to a reduction in SMB spending and associated declining transaction volumes related to the economic slowdown.

Take Rates Recover

We understand the main reason for the stock’s strong downward reaction of almost 35% decline after the previous, FY2Q 2025 earnings report was due to the drop in take rates. It appears that investors feared the take rates might be entering structural decline.

Source: company reports, analysis by the author

The graph above shows that AP/AR take rate rebounded in FY3Q to about 16.2 basis points from roughly 15.6 basis points in the previous quarter, and remained roughly in-line with the same quarter a year ago. The rebound in take rate was mainly due to growth in value added payment products such as instant transfer, pay by card, invoice financing, as well as reversal of FX related drag in the previous quarter. The Spend & Expense take rate also rebounded from roughly 258 basis points to 260 basis points on a sequential basis, and it reached roughly the level as the same quarter last year.

The recovery of take rates this quarter is evidence that the fears related to take rate sustainability that massively drove down the stock after FY2Q earnings announcement were exaggerated. Despite such recovery, we have not seen stock reaction the other way, as it was likely overshadowed by macro fears. In other words, at the moment it feels like “heads you win, and tails I lose”. Assuming the company is able to maintain its take rates (which it should over the long-term, considering the developments of ad valorem products and attach rates of such services), combined with eventual re-acceleration of the economy, the stock rebound would likely be strong as the cycle turns, in our opinion.

Macroeconomic Weakness

Much has been written and discussed in the media about the growing macroeconomic challenges; therefore, we will not spend time repeating those challenges here. Suffice it to say that we are concerned about these challenges, and they will present a headwind for most businesses, BILL included. BILL predominantly serves small and medium companies, which are more sensitive to economic cycles than large multinationals.

Considering a large customer base of over 480k businesses, as well as the processing of Total Payment Volume (TPV) of over $300BN, or almost 1% of US GDP, it should come as no surprise that BILL’s customers and therefore BILL will be impacted by the economic slowdown. With that said, BILL is well positioned to weather the downturn given that the company has a net cash position, high cash flow generation, and a fairly flexible cost structure (sales and marketing and R&D constitute over 67% of sales which can be scaled back in a recessionary environment).

Conversely, if we start to see rate cuts and other pro-growth policies coupled with a reduction of uncertainty related to tariffs, this would be a significant tailwind for BILL’s core revenue growth.

The Agentic AI Opportunity

There appears to be an important catalyst emerging on the horizon that relates to agentic AI development. Specifically, the company discussed on the earnings call its focus to develop AI agents that would help SMBs run their AP, AR, cash management and more at a high level of sophistication and efficiency without the need to incur meaningful associated labor costs. We believe the company is well positioned for success in agentic AI future because of its large scale, which affords the company an invaluable data asset. This data asset is powered by detailed customer financial data collected on its platform, where billions of dollars of payments across millions of invoices and receipts are processed.

We do not believe this opportunity is currently priced into the stock, as the market has yet to see any demo of agents, or any data to quantify its potential. With that said, the market should start pricing the agentic AI opportunity for BILL first when we see the product demo as it becomes generally available, and second, once we see attach rates start to show. While it is impossible at the moment to gauge what impact such catalysts would have, we believe it is reasonable to say they would be significant.

Compelling Valuation, Though Estimates Have Further Downside

The BILL stock is highly volatile, and responds violently to changes in growth rates. That said, we like it at current levels as the downside appears to be low based on multiples, but the upside is meaningful. The main valuation metric we use to value BILL is core revenue multiple, as well as, to a lesser extent, gross profit and adjusted operating income multiples. While the relevance of revenue multiples for rapidly growing software businesses need no justification, gross profit multiple is helpful because it allows for better comparison across different software businesses that may have varying gross margin levels. The stock trades at 11.5x FY2027 adjusted FCF multiple, which is clearly low for a strong moat, high margin, category leading SaaS business such as BILL. The tables below summarize multiple analyses based on $47.73 stock price.

The company is currently trading at 3x FY 2026 and 2.6x FY2027 core revenue, which is roughly half of historical figures for this company. It also trades at roughly half of its peer multiples (in case of FOUR, revenue multiple is much lower due to low gross margin), see table below.

The reason we look at FY2027 multiples is because, according to the company’s fiscal calendar, FY2026 starts July of this year. For this reason, starting July, the markets will increasingly start discounting FY2027 numbers in valuing the stock, and on those numbers BILL is a compelling value. As the company executes on its growth strategy, we see at least 50% upside in the stock over the holding period of 12 to 18 months, at which point would trade at more reasonable multiples of 4x core FY2027 revenue and over 17x FCF to the firm, and a share price target of about $75 per share. The growth rates should also accelerate with the re-accelerating economy and agentic AI execution by that time. Lastly, we note that our revenue estimates are below consensus estimates for both FY2026 and FY2027.

Risks

The key risk to the investment thesis is related to macroeconomic slowdown. Economic malaise affects smaller and medium businesses the most, therefore the longer and deeper the pullback, the higher the impact on SMBs. As the SMBs retrench, they tend to cut costs and payments as they do less volume, impacting BILL TPV. Furthermore, there is a possibility of take rates also being affected due to lesser usage of BILL’s ad valorem services. Lastly, typically during recessions, interest rates tend to go down meaningfully, which will act to reduce BILL’s float revenue substantially. That said, from a valuation standpoint, float revenue is not a revenue stream that we would ascribe a high multiple to.

There are certainly execution risks as well. For example, there is no certainty that the company will be able to develop agentic AI capabilities on time and on budget, nor whether attach rates of AI agents will be satisfactory. Furthermore, BILL started to expand its target market by also focusing on larger companies by adding capabilities such as multi-entity AP/AR and payments. While the larger business or mid-market business segment is attractive, BILL is facing competition from players such as AvidXchange, SAP Concur, and others, while in the SMB segment from its large rival QuickBooks (Intuit).

Lastly, our estimates are below consensus, and we expect analyst estimates to further come down as the economic slowdown plays out. While our analysis shows that at current levels downward revisions are largely priced in, we have no illusions that the stock will do well in the short term as estimates come down. As a long-term investor, though, we believe this turbulent period will provide us with ample opportunities to build a meaningful position at attractive price points for strong appreciation with eventual economic recovery.

Conclusion

In summary, while the valuation is compelling in our view, there clearly is room for consensus estimates to further come down due to macro weakness, which will likely put pressure on the stock price in the short term. We believe such macro-driven dips will be buying opportunities to build a meaningful position over-time for a rebound once the economic cycle re-accelerates within the holding period.

data.title: Omnicom Group: Poised For A Breakout With IPG Acquisition And AI-Led Transformation
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Financially, the top and bottom lines of Omnicom Group (NYSE:OMC) have stagnated. It is moderately leveraged but has no liquidity concerns since its recurring debt obligations are just a small portion of its EBITDA. Its free cash flow is consistently positive but on a mildly decreasing trend. The company is buying back shares to the benefit of shareholders.

OMC can potentially receive a boost in growth after the acquisition of Interpublic Group, which is projected to be completed in the second half of 2025. This should enable the company to benefit from multiple aspects like economies of scale in operations, diversification across sectors and greater resources to data-driven advertising. The acquisition of Interpublic can potentially increase the revenue share of some of its smaller revenue segments like "Branding & Retail Commerce" and "Healthcare".

OMC is spearheading AI-driven transformation in all aspects of its revenue through its Omni AI platform, improving advertising efficiencies. This transformation is expected to allow OMC to strengthen their value propositions, similar to other tech companies which have gone through similar AI-driven transformations.

Based on the growth guidance provided by the management, OMC appears slightly undervalued when valued using the EV/EBITDA model, offering only a very slim margin of safety.

In my opinion, the acquisition of Interpublic Group is the most promising growth potential of the company. However, it is only planned for completion in the second half of 2025. Hence, investors might want to hold and wait for the official completion of this acquisition to ensure there are no unexpected hiccups while also waiting for a higher margin of safety in its valuation.

Company Overview

Omnicom is a global marketing and corporate communications holding company that derives most of its revenue from the "Media & Advertising" segment:

It operates mostly in the North American region, although it also has a significant international presence in the European and Asia-Pacific regions:

Financials (Income Statement)

Observing its latest quarterly report, revenue improved slightly by less than 2%. Its expenses increased more than its revenue growth, resulting in its operating and net income underperforming YoY.

Looking at the long-term trend, revenue and operating income growth have stagnated in recent years since 2014, hovering around $15B.

Financials (Balance Sheet)

Operating in the advertising and marketing industry, the company typically has high receivables and payables turnover. Inventory on its balance sheet is also generally low or non-existent. These factors led to a low working capital position.

With a total debt that is close to 2.5x its estimated EBITDA, OMC is moderately leveraged. However, there are no immediate liquidity concerns as its recurring net interest expense on its debt is only a small fraction of its EBITDA:

Financials (Cash Flow)

Observing its annual historical trend, OMC's FCF appears to be mildly declining:

With a gradient of -64.3, its FCF declines at an average rate of $64.3MM per year. Relative to its latest FCF of $1418.2 in the latest TTM, this is a decrease of approximately 4.53% annually.

Valuation

The company guided EBITDA as "10 basis points higher than 15.5%", implying a forward EBITDA margin of 15.6%:

Together with the projected organic growth range expected of its revenue, the forward revenue and EBITDA for 2025 are derived as follows:

From Seeking Alpha's comparison with OMC's peers, the average EV/EBITDA multiple is derived as follows:

Together with other information from the latest quarterly report, we derived the intrinsic value as follows:

The current price of OMC appears slightly undervalued.

Acquisition Of Interpublic Group

The company announced the acquisition of Interpublic Group (IPG) in December 2024 and is on track to complete the transaction in the second half of 2025.

The acquisition creates the largest advertising holding company in the world and synergistically integrates multiple major advertising networks from both OMC and IPG together:

The merged entities offer OMC several benefits:

Economies of Scale:

The immediate benefit of this synergy can be observed from the estimated $750MM worth of annual cost savings:

As noted by VideoWeek, OMC and IPG have significant overlaps across multiple revenue segments. This enables its back-office and agency infrastructure to share operations, allowing the combined entity to realize the expected cost efficiencies.

Benefiting from Data-Driven advertising services:

As noted by VideoWeek, gaining control of IPG's data advertising capabilities is among the more important benefits for OMC since it does not have a "comparable asset" in this area:

Having been named "Creative Holding Company of the Year" in "The One Show 2023" for the 6th time within the past decade, Omnicom excels in higher-end, creative branding solutions. IPG, on the other hand, boasts a strong roster of marketing service companies that are more data and tech-centric.

So, it's not hard to see how Omnicom can look forward to benefiting from IPG's track record in leveraging data and technology to add meaningful value to its offering in an advertising landscape that is increasingly populated by digital-native competitors.

Sector Diversification:

IPG owns 'IPG Health' which consists of 45 agencies that have worked with notable players in the pharmaceutical industry:

As noted earlier, healthcare is one of the smallest revenues by segment. Hence, the acquisition allows OMC to diversify away from its largest segment of "Media & Advertising" to other smaller segments.

Strength in Media With Challenges in Branding

As highlighted in the latest earnings call, the "Media and Advertising" segment improved in the latest quarter with the improvements largely contributed by the "Media" portion:

In contrast, the "Branding & Retail Commerce" segment struggled, with the decline led by the "Branding" portion:

If we look at the numbers in perspective, "Media and Advertising" is already by far the largest segment of OMC, accounting for more than half of total revenue, while the "Branding & Retail Commerce" segment is one of the smallest:

Despite the smallest segment being down by more than 14%, the close to 5% revenue increase from its largest segment more than made up for it, with its total revenue increasing marginally by close to 2%.

As noted from the comparison pie charts from VideoWeek, IPG's "Branding & Retail Commerce" segment appears to contribute more revenue than OMC. Therefore, IPG should significantly contribute to OMC's smaller "Branding & Retail Commerce" segment after the acquisition.

AI-Driven Transformation Across Omnicom's Business Units

OMC is leveraging AI in every aspect of its operations:

Its leverage of AI is spearheaded by its proprietary Omni AI platform:

While Omni AI is already used by many, its uses are mostly restricted to employees, and OMC is aiming to extend its access to its clients as well:

Omni AI brings together data from different sources, making it accessible under a real-time unified view. AI-driven insights reduce guesswork for customers, minimising the time to make marketing decisions and maximising results and accuracy. Overall, it has the potential to improve advertising efficiencies compared to accessing different data sources in silos and making decisions based on individualized experiences.

The use of AI is not uncommon in companies with forward-looking growth strategies. Large tech companies like Microsoft already integrated AI into their office applications to strengthen their value propositions. If executed successfully, OMC can reap the same benefits.

Shareholder Returns

The company has been repurchasing shares to the benefit of shareholders:

Historically, its return on equity ('ROE') and total capital ('ROTC') have been maintained at a high rate of above 20% and 12% respectively in most years:

Conclusion

In my opinion, the most compelling growth opportunity can be observed in the ongoing acquisition of IPG. This strategic move, once completed, has the potential to materially boost OMC's revenue, especially in the smaller revenue segments.

AI-driven transformation should also materially contribute to the company's growth in the long run, but it might take longer to materialize.

However, unexpected events might still occur that put a halt to the acquisition. Since the current valuation offers limited upside potential, investors might wish to consider waiting for a better valuation to present itself while observing whether OMC can follow through with its ongoing acquisition of IPG to completion.
